title: Scoped enumerations (enum classes)

exercises.title: Traffic Light with Scoped Enum
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Model a traffic light using a scoped enumeration and print the action a driver should take for each light color.

## Input
- A single line containing an integer: `0` for red, `1` for yellow, `2` for green.

## Output
Print exactly one line in this format:
```
<Color>: <Action>
```
Where `<Color>` is `Red`, `Yellow`, or `Green`, and `<Action>` is `Stop`, `Caution`, or `Go` respectively.

If the input is not 0, 1, or 2, print:
```
Unknown: Invalid
```

## Requirements
1. Define a scoped enumeration `enum class Light` with enumerators `red`, `yellow`, `green` (in that order, starting from default value 0).
2. Write a function `std::string lightToString(Light l)` that returns the capitalized color name (`"Red"`, `"Yellow"`, `"Green"`). Use a `switch` statement. Return `"Unknown"` for any unrecognized value.
3. Write a function `std::string lightToAction(Light l)` that returns the action (`"Stop"`, `"Caution"`, `"Go"`). Use a `switch` statement. Return `"Invalid"` for any unrecognized value.
4. In `main`, read an integer from `std::cin`, convert it to a `Light` using `static_cast`, then print the result using the two functions.
5. All enumerator access must use scope resolution (e.g., `Light::red`). Do not use unscoped enums.

## Notes
- You may use `#include <iostream>` and `#include <string>`.

exercises.title: Permission Checker with Explicit Underlying Type
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Use a scoped enumeration with an explicit underlying type to model file permissions, then check which permissions a user has.

## Input
- Line 1: a single integer representing a combined permission value (0-7).
  - `1` = read, `2` = write, `4` = execute
  - Values combine additively: `3` = read+write, `5` = read+execute, `6` = write+execute, `7` = all three, `0` = none.

## Output
Print exactly three lines, one per permission, in this order:
```
Read: <yes/no>
Write: <yes/no>
Execute: <yes/no>
```
Print `yes` if the permission is present in the input value, `no` otherwise.

## Requirements
1. Define `enum class Permission : int` with enumerators `read = 1`, `write = 2`, `execute = 4`.
2. Write a function `bool hasPermission(int mask, Permission p)` that returns `true` if the permission bit is set. Use `static_cast<int>(p)` to extract the underlying value, then use bitwise AND (`&`) to test the bit.
3. In `main`, read the integer mask from `std::cin`, then call `hasPermission` for each of the three permissions in order (read, write, execute).
4. Print `yes` or `no` based on the return value. Use the exact output format shown above.
5. All enumerator access must use scope resolution (e.g., `Permission::read`).

## Notes
- You may use `#include <iostream>` and `#include <string>`.
- The input will always be an integer in the range 0-7.

test_cases.label: Sample — all permissions (7)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Read: yes
Write: yes
Execute: yes

test_cases.label: Hidden — read and execute only (5)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Read: yes
Write: no
Execute: yes

test_cases.label: Hidden — no permissions (0)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Read: no
Write: no
Execute: no
